LOOK.text: Each server has a label: 'FloorEngine', 'TraitLab', 'SweepBot'. LEDs blink furiously. These servers are running Adrian's entire ecosystem. Every NFT render, every floor sweep, every trait swap flows through here.

USE.text: I touch a server. It's warm. Really warm. Like holding a phone that's been running a DEX aggregator for 6 hours.

TALK.text: The servers hum in unison. A low drone. The sound of thousands of transactions being indexed. It's almost... musical.

PICK.text: I can't take a server rack. That would literally rug the entire ecosystem.

OPEN.text: I pop open a server panel. Cables, dust, and a sticky note: 'DO NOT REBOOT ON TUESDAYS.' No explanation given.

CLOSE.text: I close the panel. The servers keep running. Uptime is everything.

PUSH.text: I push a server blade back into its slot. The rack shudders. A monitoring alert fires somewhere. Oops.

PULL.text: I pull a server blade slightly out. The 'SweepBot' LED goes red. I shove it back in immediately. Crisis averted.

GIVE.text: The servers demand electricity, not gifts. Although more RAM wouldn't hurt.

LOOK.text: This rack is completely dead. No lights, no hum. The label reads 'AdrianAuctions'. A burn mark on the chassis tells the story. Overheated during a bidding war. Never recovered.

USE.text: I flip the power switch. Nothing. Not even a spark. This server gave its life during the great auction frenzy of '24.

TALK.text: Silence. The dead rack speaks no more. A moment of silence for fallen infrastructure.

else.text: I pry out a fused processor. The heat from that bidding war literally melted it. A relic of unchecked on-chain ambition.

OPEN.text: I open the panel. Charred components, a melted SSD, and the faint smell of burnt silicon. The hard drives are fused together.

CLOSE.text: I close the panel. Rest in peace, AdrianAuctions server. You burned bright. Too bright.

PUSH.text: I push the dead rack. It tips slightly. Empty inside. All the data is gone. Like tears in rain.

PULL.text: I pull open the backup drive bay. Empty. Whoever ran this server believed in living dangerously. No backups.

GIVE.text: This server is beyond saving. Not even a full hardware refresh could bring it back. Like most projects after a bear market.

LOOK.text: Active monitoring logs. FloorEngine, TraitLab, SweepBot — all Adrian's infrastructure, running autonomously.

else.text: Downloaded. The last entry reads: 'PZ discharge protocol initiated. All records moved to treatment room.'

TALK.text: The terminal beeps. In Morse code it spells: 'GET OUT OF MY SERVER ROOM.' Rude.

else.text: Downloaded. The last entry reads: 'PZ discharge protocol initiated. All records moved to treatment room.'

OPEN.text: I type 'open sesame'. The terminal responds: 'This is a production server, not a fairy tale. Try sudo.' I don't have sudo.

CLOSE.text: I type 'exit'. The terminal responds: 'Goodbye. Please close the door on your way out. Actually, don't. There is no door.'

PUSH.text: I push Enter repeatedly. The terminal fills with error messages. Classic sysadmin move.

PULL.text: I pull up the command history. Someone typed 'git push --force' at 3 AM. That explains a lot.

GIVE.text: The terminal only accepts valid commands. And occasionally profanity, which it ignores silently.

LOOK.text: An industrial cooling unit rattling against the wall. Water drips from a pipe joint. This must be the source of the stains on the basement ceiling upstairs.

USE.text: I turn the temperature dial. The unit groans louder. The servers seem happier. Like overclocked GPUs with a water cooler.

TALK.text: The cooling system speaks in rattles and drips. The language of entropy. Of heat death. Of servers running too hot for too long.

PICK.text: I catch a drop of water from the leak. Cold as a bear market winter. Refreshing.

OPEN.text: I open the maintenance panel. Coolant pipes, a filter that hasn't been changed in years, and a family of spiders who've made this their DeFi protocol.

CLOSE.text: I close the panel. The rattling continues. Some things just can't be fixed.

PUSH.text: I push the unit. More water leaks. That's probably not good. Like pushing a button in production.

PULL.text: I pull the drip tray out. It's full. I empty it into... the floor. Where else? There's no drain down here.

GIVE.text: The cooling system needs a plumber, not a player character. But I appreciate the sentiment.

then.text: The switch is flipped. The hidden screen still glows with coordinates: 47.6062N, 122.3321W. Patient Zero Rehabilitation Clinic.

else.text: A big red switch behind a glass panel mounted low on the wall. The label reads: 'EMERGENCY SHUTOFF - DO NOT TOUCH.' ...but it's right there. Taunting me.

then.text: The switch is already flipped. The hidden screen on the wall still shows those coordinates. Whatever they mean.

else.text: I flip the switch. Everything goes dark for a moment... The servers die. Then emergency generators kick in with a deep rumble.
Behind the glass: an emergency kill switch. But someone repurposed it.
A hidden screen flickers on. Coordinates. A name: 'Patient Zero Rehabilitation Clinic'.
Adrian didn't just build infrastructure. He built an escape route.
Coordinates: 47.6062N, 122.3321W. The clinic. That's where I need to go.
The mountain path from outside the lab... it must lead there.
The switch is behind glass. I'd need to break it first. The glass, I mean. Not the switch. Or the servers.

TALK.text: I lean close to the switch. 'Should I?' The switch doesn't answer. Switches never do. They just flip.

PICK.text: The switch is mounted to the wall. I can't take it. I can only flip it. If I dare.

OPEN.text: The glass panel is sealed. No hinge, no latch. Break it or leave it. Like most smart contracts.

CLOSE.text: Nothing to close here. The glass is either intact or broken. There is no in-between.

then.text: The glass is already broken. The switch is exposed. Just USE it if I dare.

else.text: I push the glass panel. *CRACK* It shatters. Glass everywhere. The red switch is exposed now, practically begging to be flipped. Do I dare?

PULL.text: I pull at the glass frame. It doesn't budge. Pushing might work better. Or a good punch.

GIVE.text: The emergency switch doesn't accept gifts. It only accepts reckless decisions.

LOOK.text: A thick cable conduit drops from the ceiling. Fiber optic, copper, and something that glows faintly blue. The blockchain's umbilical cord.

USE.text: I trace the cables. They come from the lab above and feed every server in this room. Cut this and the whole ecosystem goes dark.

TALK.text: The cables hum with data. Millions of transactions per second, reduced to a gentle vibration. The sound of DeFi sleeping.

PICK.text: I'm not yanking cables out of a live server room. I may be reckless, but I'm not an ISP technician.

OPEN.text: I peel back the conduit sheath. Inside: a rainbow of Cat6, fiber optic, and one wire labeled 'DO NOT TOUCH - BOB.' Who's Bob?

CLOSE.text: I wrap the sheath back. The conduit looks as good as new. Which is to say, terrible.

PUSH.text: I push the conduit. It sways gently. Somewhere, a transaction probably just timed out.

PULL.text: I tug a loose cable. The terminal screen flickers. I let go VERY quickly.

GIVE.text: The conduit doesn't want gifts. It wants bandwidth. Always more bandwidth.

LOOK.text: Standard raised server room floor. Perforated tiles for airflow. Under them: more cables than a DeFi protocol has dependencies.

USE.text: I stomp on a tile. It shifts slightly. Cold air rushes up from below. Nature's air conditioning, funded by electricity bills.

TALK.text: My footsteps echo on the raised floor. Each step sounds like a block confirmation. Tap. Tap. Finalized.

PICK.text: I lift a floor tile. Below: a rat's nest of cables, a forgotten USB drive, and what appears to be a half-eaten sandwich from 2023.

OPEN.text: I pry open a tile. The underfloor is like Ethereum's mempool — chaotic, congested, and full of things waiting to be processed.

CLOSE.text: I put the tile back. It clicks into place with satisfying finality. Like closing a position in profit. Rare but satisfying.

PUSH.text: I push a tile sideways. It grinds against its neighbor. The floor was not designed for this kind of interaction.

PULL.text: I pull up a floor tile. A cold wind blows from below. The server room's own liquidity undercurrent.

GIVE.text: The floor tiles accept only gravity. And the occasional dropped screw.

LOOK.text: Faded blueprints etched into the wall. Network diagrams, IP ranges, and a hand-drawn map labeled 'ADRIAN INFRASTRUCTURE v0.1'. This is where it all began.

USE.text: I trace the diagram with my finger. Every server, every bot, every contract — all mapped out. Adrian was meticulous. Obsessively so.

TALK.text: The schematics don't talk. But they tell a story. One of scale, ambition, and way too many server processes running at once.

PICK.text: They're etched into the wall. Like on-chain data — permanent and impossible to take with you.

OPEN.text: I look closer. One section is labeled 'PHASE 2: PATIENT ZERO.' It's circled three times. Whatever that phase is, it mattered.

CLOSE.text: I step back from the schematics. Some things are better viewed from a distance. Like my portfolio.

PUSH.text: I push on the wall panel. It's solid. These schematics aren't going anywhere. Literally immutable.

PULL.text: I try to peel the blueprint off. It's bonded to the wall. Permanent deployment, no rollback possible.

GIVE.text: The wall schematics are read-only. No write access for visitors.
